_provenance: Claude-authored original CET-6 careful reading, batch E2. ~280-340 word academic/social passages, 4 MCQs each (detail/inference/main idea/vocabulary-in-context/attitude). DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: Does the language we speak shape the way we think? The question has long divided scholars. An extreme version of the idea — that language imprisons thought, so that speakers of different languages inhabit fundamentally different mental worlds — has been largely discredited. Yet a more modest claim is gaining experimental support: that the particular language we speak can subtly influence how we perceive and remember the world. Consider colour. Languages divide the spectrum differently; some have a single word covering both blue and green, while others, like Russian, have separate basic terms for light and dark blue. Experiments show that Russian speakers are measurably faster at distinguishing shades that fall across this linguistic boundary, as though their language had sharpened their perception at that point. Grammar, too, may leave its mark. Some languages require speakers to specify, in the structure of every sentence, information that others leave vague — the exact source of a piece of knowledge, for instance, or the precise spatial relationship between objects. Speakers of such languages, research suggests, become habitually more attentive to these details, simply because their language obliges them to. None of this means that thought is trapped by language; we can all grasp concepts our language lacks a word for. But it does suggest that language is not merely a neutral vehicle for thought, a transparent window onto a reality we all perceive identically. It is, rather, one of the lenses through which we view the world — a lens we acquire so early, and wear so constantly, that we rarely notice it is there at all. To become aware of that lens, whether by learning another language or simply by reflecting on our own, is to glimpse, however briefly, just how much of what we take for plain, objective reality is in fact quietly shaped by the particular words we happen to have inherited.

questions.prompt: What modest claim is gaining experimental support?
options: That the language we speak can subtly influence how we perceive and remember.
That language has no effect on thought.

questions.prompt: What does the colour example show?
options: That Russian speakers distinguish certain shades faster because their language separates them.
That colour words are identical everywhere.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, 'discredited' most nearly means
How does the writer describe language?
options: As a neutral, transparent window onto reality.
As one of the lenses through which we view the world.
As a prison for all thought.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 语言可微妙影响感知与记忆)、细节(B 俄语者因语言区分而辨色更快)、词义(B discredited=被证伪)、主旨(B 语言是我们观看世界的透镜之一)。

sets.passage: The 'sharing economy' arrived with utopian promises. By using technology to let ordinary people rent out their spare rooms, cars or time, it would, we were told, reduce waste, foster community, and allow people to earn money from assets that would otherwise sit idle. Reality, as so often, has proved more complicated. Some of the benefits are real. Such platforms have indeed made many goods and services cheaper and more convenient, and have allowed some people to earn useful income. Yet critics point out that the language of 'sharing' obscures what is, in most cases, simply a new form of commerce — one that has often grown by avoiding the regulations and protections that govern traditional businesses. The consequences fall unevenly. The companies that own the platforms have captured enormous wealth, while the workers who provide the actual labour frequently lack the security, benefits and protections of conventional employment, bearing risks that employers once carried. What was sold as flexibility can, for many, mean insecurity. There are wider effects too. In popular cities, the conversion of homes into short-term rentals for visitors has been blamed for reducing the supply of housing for residents and driving up rents. A technology promising to let people share has, in some places, made it harder for locals to find a place to live. None of this means the sharing economy is simply bad. But it illustrates a recurring pattern: that a technology's social effects depend less on the technology itself than on the rules within which it operates, and on who, in the end, captures its rewards. The very same platform, governed by different rules, could either spread its benefits widely or concentrate them in a very few hands; the technology by itself decides neither outcome, and to pretend that it does is to quietly surrender a choice that properly belongs to society as a whole.

questions.prompt: What did the sharing economy originally promise?
options: To reduce waste, foster community, and let people earn from idle assets.

questions.prompt: What do critics say the language of 'sharing' obscures?
options: A new form of commerce that often avoids regulation.

questions.prompt: How do the consequences fall?
options: Unevenly: platforms capture wealth while workers bear risk and insecurity.

questions.prompt: What recurring pattern does the passage illustrate?
options: That a technology's effects depend on its rules and on who captures its rewards.
That cities should ban all rentals.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 减浪费/促社区/盘活闲置资产)、细节(B 实为规避监管的新型商业)、细节(B 平台获利、劳动者担险不均)、主旨(B 技术影响取决于规则与谁获回报)。

sets.passage: For centuries, sleep was viewed as a passive state, a mere suspension of activity during which the brain, like the body, simply rested. Modern neuroscience has overturned this picture entirely. The sleeping brain, we now know, is intensely active, and much of what it does is essential to memory and learning. During the day, the brain encodes new experiences in a fragile, temporary form. It is largely during sleep that these memories are processed and consolidated — strengthened, organised, and transferred into more stable, long-term storage. This is why sleep after learning is so valuable: a student who sleeps after studying will typically retain far more than one who stays awake, however diligently the latter revises. But sleep does more than merely strengthen memories; it appears to reshape them. As we sleep, the brain replays and recombines the day's experiences, extracting patterns, forging connections between previously unrelated pieces of information, and integrating new knowledge with old. This may explain the common experience of waking with the solution to a problem that had seemed intractable the night before. Sleep, in a sense, is when the brain makes sense of what it has learned. The implications reach beyond the classroom. A culture that treats sleep as expendable, to be sacrificed for work or entertainment, may be undermining the very mental faculties it most prizes. To sleep, the science increasingly suggests, is not to switch the mind off, but to allow it to perform some of its most important work. Seen in this light, the familiar advice to 'sleep on it' before making a hard decision is not idle folk wisdom but sound neuroscience, and the modern habit of trading away sleep for an extra hour of work may quietly cost us a great deal more than it ever appears to gain.

questions.prompt: How was sleep formerly viewed?
options: As a passive state of mere rest.

questions.prompt: What does sleep do for memory?
options: It consolidates memories into stable, long-term storage.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, 'intractable' most nearly means
What does the writer warn about?
options: That sleep is a waste of time.
That treating sleep as expendable may undermine the mind's faculties.
That we should sleep far less.
That sleep is only physical rest.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 被动的纯休息)、细节(B 巩固记忆至长期存储)、词义(B intractable=难解的)、主旨(B 视睡眠为可牺牲会损害心智)。

sets.passage: Under the right conditions, a crowd can be astonishingly wise. Ask a large group to estimate the number of sweets in a jar, and while individual guesses vary wildly, their average is often startlingly close to the truth. This 'wisdom of crowds' arises because individual errors, when they are independent, tend to cancel one another out, leaving the collective judgement remarkably accurate. But the phenomenon depends on a crucial and fragile condition: that the individuals judge independently. The moment people begin to influence one another — to see others' answers before giving their own — the magic can evaporate. Instead of a diverse collection of independent estimates, the group may collapse into conformity, all echoing the same opinion, including the same mistakes. This helps explain why crowds can also be spectacularly foolish. Financial bubbles, fashions, and waves of collective panic all arise when people, instead of thinking for themselves, take their cues from those around them. Each individual, reasonably enough, assumes that if everyone else is doing something, they must know something. But when everyone is merely copying everyone else, the group's apparent confidence rests on nothing at all. The lesson is subtle. Collective judgement is not reliably wise or foolish in itself; its quality depends on the conditions under which it forms. To harness the wisdom of a crowd, paradoxically, we must protect the independence of its members — encouraging diverse, private judgement rather than the comfortable consensus that so often passes for wisdom but is, in truth, merely an echo. The wise crowd and the foolish mob, in other words, are not two different kinds of people at all, but the very same people under different conditions, and the whole difference between them lies in whether each member is left genuinely free to reach a judgement of their own.

questions.prompt: Why can a crowd be wise?
options: Because everyone in it is an expert.
Because independent individual errors tend to cancel out.

questions.prompt: What fragile condition does the wisdom of crowds depend on?
Why can crowds be foolish?
options: Because people take cues from others instead of thinking for themselves.

questions.prompt: What is the paradoxical lesson?
options: That to harness a crowd's wisdom we must protect its members' independence.
That consensus is always true wisdom.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 独立误差相互抵消)、细节(B 个体独立判断)、细节(B 人们盲从他人而非独立思考)、主旨(B 要善用群体智慧需保护成员独立性)。

sets.passage: The belief that contact with nature is good for us is ancient, but only recently has science begun to measure just how profound its effects may be. A growing body of research suggests that access to green space — parks, gardens, even tree-lined streets — has measurable benefits for both physical and mental health, benefits significant enough that some now regard green space not as an urban luxury but as a public-health necessity. The evidence is striking. Studies following large populations have found that people living near green space report lower levels of stress, anxiety and depression than otherwise similar neighbours without such access. Remarkably, these benefits do not seem to depend on how often people actually exercise outdoors; merely living near nature, and perhaps simply seeing it, appears to help. Researchers have proposed several explanations. Green spaces may encourage social contact and physical activity; they dampen noise and cool the air; and they may offer the mind a form of restful, undemanding attention that relieves the mental fatigue of urban life. Whatever the precise mechanism, the effect is real and consistent. The policy implications are clear, and urgent. As the world's population grows ever more urban, planners who treat parks and trees as optional decorations — the first items to be cut when budgets tighten — may be making a false economy. If green space genuinely protects mental and physical health, then ensuring that everyone, regardless of wealth, has access to it is not a matter of beautification, but of justice and public health alike. To plant trees and protect parks in the poorest and most crowded districts, on this view, is not a pleasant extravagance to be indulged only in good times, but one of the cheapest and most equitable investments in public well-being that any city can possibly make.

questions.prompt: What does the research suggest about green space?
options: That it has measurable health benefits and may be a public-health necessity.
That it is harmful to health.

questions.prompt: What is striking about the benefits?
options: They appear even without frequent outdoor exercise.

questions.prompt: As used in the passage, 'false economy' most nearly means
options: an apparent saving that actually costs more later.

questions.prompt: What does the writer argue access to green space is a matter of?

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 有可量化的身心益处、或为公共卫生必需)、细节(B 不依赖常户外锻炼也有益)、词义(B false economy=看似省实则更费)、主旨(B 是公平与公共卫生问题)。

sets.passage: Why are living things ever kind to one another? At first glance, altruism — behaving in a way that benefits another at a cost to oneself — seems to contradict the logic of evolution, which is often crudely summarised as 'survival of the fittest'. If life is a ruthless competition, how could selflessness ever survive? Biologists have uncovered several answers. The first is kinship. Because relatives share genes, helping a close relative survive and reproduce can, in evolutionary terms, be a way of promoting one's own genes. A creature that sacrifices itself for several siblings may, paradoxically, leave more copies of its genes behind than one that selfishly survives alone. A second answer is reciprocity. Among animals that live in stable groups and recognise one another, helping another can pay off if the favour is later returned, provided cheats who take without giving can be detected and excluded. In humans, these mechanisms are overlaid with something further: a concern for reputation. Because we live in communities where information about our behaviour spreads, being known as generous and trustworthy brings real advantages, while a reputation for selfishness carries lasting costs. None of this reduces human kindness to mere calculation; we experience compassion as genuine, and it is. But it does dissolve the apparent paradox. Far from contradicting evolution, altruism, under the right conditions, is exactly what a clear-eyed understanding of evolution would predict. The deep human capacity for cooperation, then, is not a fragile exception forever struggling against our supposedly selfish nature, but is itself a direct product of that nature, written into us by the very same evolutionary logic that shaped everything else about us. Kindness, on this account, is woven into our nature precisely because, in the long run, it once helped our distant ancestors to survive and to flourish together.

questions.prompt: Why does altruism seem to contradict evolution?
options: Because it benefits another at a cost to oneself, seeming to oppose 'survival of the fittest'.

questions.prompt: How does kinship help explain altruism?
options: Helping relatives, who share one's genes, can promote one's own genes.

questions.prompt: What extra mechanism operates in humans?
What does the writer conclude about altruism and evolution?
options: That, far from contradicting evolution, altruism is what it would predict.
That kindness is merely cold calculation.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 利己逻辑下自损利他似矛盾)、细节(B 助共享基因的亲属即促己基因)、细节(B 人类还多了声誉考量)、主旨(B 利他恰是进化所预测的)。

sets.passage: Misinformation is as old as communication itself, but the digital age has given it unprecedented speed and reach. Understanding why false information spreads so readily — and why it often outpaces the truth — has become one of the urgent questions of our time. Part of the answer lies in the nature of falsehood itself. Freed from the constraint of being true, a fabricated story can be crafted to be maximally surprising, emotionally charged, and shareable. One large study found that false news stories spread significantly faster and more widely than true ones, precisely because they were more novel and provoked stronger emotions, which prompt people to share. The design of the platforms compounds the problem. Their algorithms, built to maximise engagement, tend to amplify whatever provokes the strongest reaction, with little regard for whether it is accurate. Outrage and sensation are, from the algorithm's point of view, simply effective. But the deepest source of the problem may lie in ourselves. Through a tendency known as confirmation bias, we are inclined to accept uncritically information that fits our existing beliefs, and to dismiss what challenges them. False information that flatters our prejudices slips past our defences with ease. This suggests that the problem cannot be solved by removing false content alone, for new falsehoods will always appear. The more durable defence is to cultivate, in ourselves and others, the habits of critical thinking: to pause before sharing, to consider the source, and to be especially sceptical of information that we are most eager to believe. The hardest and most valuable discipline of all, in the end, is to turn that scepticism not outward upon our opponents, whom we find easy enough to doubt, but inward upon our own most cherished and most comfortable convictions.

questions.prompt: Why can false stories spread so fast?
options: Because, freed from truth, they can be made maximally surprising and emotional.

questions.prompt: How do platform algorithms compound the problem?
options: They amplify whatever provokes the strongest reaction, regardless of accuracy.

questions.prompt: What is confirmation bias?
options: Accepting uncritically what fits our beliefs and dismissing what challenges them.

questions.prompt: What is the more durable defence against misinformation?

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 不受真相约束、可极尽惊奇与情绪)、细节(B 算法放大最具反应的内容)、细节(B 确认偏误:信合己见、弃异见)、主旨(B 更持久的防线是培养批判性思维)。

sets.passage: What makes work meaningful? In an age when many fear that automation will reduce the quantity of work, a quieter question concerns its quality: why some jobs feel worthwhile and others empty, and what, beyond a pay cheque, work can give us. Research into the psychology of work suggests that meaningful work tends to share certain features. The first is a sense that one's efforts make a genuine difference — that the work has some positive effect on others, however indirect. A second is autonomy: the freedom to exercise judgement and to shape how one's work is done, rather than merely following orders. A third is the opportunity to develop and use one's skills, to be stretched and to grow. Strikingly, money, beyond a level sufficient for security, seems to contribute relatively little to whether work feels meaningful. People will often choose lower-paid work they find purposeful over better-paid work they find hollow, and those in well-paid but meaningless jobs frequently report a peculiar sense of emptiness. This has implications both personal and organisational. For individuals, it suggests that in choosing work, the search for meaning may matter as much as the search for income. For employers, it implies that productivity and well-being alike may depend less on financial incentives than on whether work is designed to be genuinely engaging — to give people autonomy, growth, and a sense that what they do matters. A job, it seems, is not merely a means of earning a living, but, for better or worse, a large part of how we spend, and experience, our lives. To ask what makes work meaningful is therefore not a soft or secondary question at all, but one that bears directly on the quality of the long years that most of us will spend labouring, and on whether those years come to feel, in the end, like a life genuinely lived or merely endured.

questions.prompt: What features does meaningful work tend to share?
options: Making a difference, autonomy, and the chance to use and grow one's skills.

questions.prompt: What role does money play beyond a level sufficient for security?
options: Relatively little in whether work feels meaningful.
No role in anything at all.
It is the only thing that matters.

questions.prompt: What do people in well-paid but meaningless jobs often report?
What does this imply for employers?
options: That pay is all that matters.
That productivity and well-being depend on designing genuinely engaging work.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 有影响、自主、能用并成长技能)、细节(B 超过安全线后金钱对意义贡献甚微)、细节(B 高薪却空洞者常感空虚)、主旨(B 雇主应设计真正有吸引力的工作)。

sets.passage: For most of medical history, microbes were seen chiefly as enemies — the germs that cause disease, to be killed wherever possible. In recent years, a scientific revolution has transformed this picture. We now understand that the human body is home to trillions of microbes, collectively known as the microbiome, and that far from being merely harmful, many of them are essential to our health. These microorganisms, most of them living in our gut, perform a remarkable range of functions. They help us digest food and produce vitamins we cannot make ourselves. They train and regulate our immune system, teaching it to distinguish friend from foe. Some research even suggests they influence our brain and mood, communicating with the nervous system in ways only beginning to be understood. Crucially, the composition of this inner ecosystem appears to matter greatly. A diverse, balanced microbiome is associated with good health, while a disturbed one has been linked to a range of conditions, from digestive disorders to obesity and allergies. Modern life, with its processed foods and overuse of antibiotics, may be impoverishing our internal ecosystems, with consequences we are only starting to grasp. This new understanding has profound implications. It suggests that health depends not only on the body itself, but on the vast community of organisms it hosts. To care for ourselves, it turns out, we must also care for the invisible multitude within us — a humbling reminder that the boundary between 'us' and 'them', between the human and the microbial, is far less clear than we once supposed. We are, it now appears, less solitary individuals than walking ecosystems, vast and teeming communities of cooperating organisms, and learning to tend that hidden inner community wisely may yet prove one of the more surprising frontiers of twenty-first-century medicine.

questions.prompt: How were microbes formerly seen?
options: As enemies, germs to be killed wherever possible.

questions.prompt: What functions does the microbiome perform?
options: Helping digestion, producing vitamins, and training the immune system.

questions.prompt: What is a diverse, balanced microbiome associated with?
What does the passage suggest about the boundary between 'us' and 'them'?
options: That it is sharp and clear.
That the boundary between human and microbial is far less clear than once supposed.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 曾被视为应消灭的病菌)、细节(B 助消化、产维生素、训练免疫)、细节(B 多样均衡的菌群关联健康)、主旨(B 人与微生物的界线远比想象模糊)。

sets.passage: Much has been written about the physical effects of climate change — rising seas, fiercer storms, shifting seasons. Far less attention has been paid to its psychological effects, yet these may prove profound, shaping how individuals and societies respond to the crisis. For those directly affected by climate disasters, the mental toll is clear: trauma, grief, and lasting anxiety. But the psychological effects extend further, to many who have suffered no direct harm. Researchers have begun to study what some call 'climate anxiety' — a chronic, low-level distress about the future of the planet, reported especially among the young, who will live longest with the consequences. A particular psychological challenge is that climate change is, in many ways, poorly suited to engaging the human mind. It is gradual, abstract, global and statistical, whereas our emotions evolved to respond to threats that are immediate, concrete, local and personal. A slowly rising average temperature simply does not trigger the alarm that a charging predator does. This mismatch helps explain the strange gap between the scale of the danger and the weakness of our collective response. Understanding these psychological dimensions is not a distraction from the practical work of addressing climate change; it is essential to it. How we communicate the crisis, how we cope with fear without sliding into paralysing despair or denial, and how we sustain hope and motivation over the long term are not soft questions at the margins, but central to whether humanity can muster the sustained, collective will that the problem demands. A movement that ignores how people actually feel, offering only frightening statistics with no answering sense of hope or agency, may in the end do quite as much to paralyse as to mobilise, and the science of the human mind may prove every bit as important here as the science of the atmosphere itself.

questions.prompt: What aspect of climate change has received less attention?
What is 'climate anxiety'?
options: A chronic, low-level distress about the planet's future.

questions.prompt: Why is climate change poorly suited to engaging the mind?
options: Because it is too local and personal.
Because it is gradual and abstract, while our emotions respond to immediate, concrete threats.

questions.prompt: Why does understanding the psychology matter, according to the writer?
options: It is essential to sustaining the collective will the problem demands.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 心理影响被忽视)、细节(B 对地球未来的慢性低度焦虑)、细节(B 渐进抽象、而情绪只对即时具体威胁警觉)、主旨(B 理解心理对凝聚持久集体意志至关重要)。
